## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Arizona? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/arizona.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Arizona?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Arizona: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Arizona

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Arizona

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Arizona:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Arizona)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Arizona

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Arizona, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Arizona has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Arizona.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Arizona is approximately $65,014 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Arizona.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Arizona exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Arizona?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Arizona is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Arizona?

Chapter 13 in Arizona costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Arizona?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Arkansas? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/arkansas.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Arkansas?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Arkansas: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Arkansas

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Arkansas

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Arkansas:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Arkansas)$1,000 - $2,000$2,500 - $4,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,368 - $2,438$2,843 - $4,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Arkansas

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Arkansas, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,000 - $2,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $2,500 - $4,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Arkansas has informal fee standards. Districts: Eastern District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,368 - $2,438$2,843 - $4,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Chapter 13: $313

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Arkansas is approximately $54,714 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,368 - $2,438 in Arkansas.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Arkansas exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Arkansas?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Arkansas is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,000 - $2,000 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Arkansas?

Chapter 13 in Arkansas costs $313 in filing fees plus $2,500 - $4,500 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Arkansas?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Missouri ·
Tennessee ·
Mississippi ·
Louisiana ·
Texas ·
Oklahoma

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in California? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/california.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in California?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in California: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › California

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in California

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for California:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (California)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in California

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In California, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,500 - $3,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,500 - $5,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in California has informal fee standards. Districts: Central District, Eastern District, Northern District, Southern District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for California is approximately $79,253 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,868 - $3,438 in California.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review California exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in California?

Total Chapter 7 cost in California is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,500 - $3,000 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in California?

Chapter 13 in California costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,500 - $5,500 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in California?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Oregon ·
Nevada ·
Arizona

California Exemptions ·
How to File in California ·
File Without a Lawyer ·

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Colorado? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/colorado.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Attorney Fees

By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Colorado?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Colorado: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Colorado

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Colorado

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Colorado:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Colorado)$2,000 - $3,500$4,000 - $6,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Colorado

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Colorado, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $2,000 - $3,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $4,000 - $6,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Colorado has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Colorado.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Colorado is approximately $82,700 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $2,368 - $3,938 in Colorado.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Connecticut? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/connecticut.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Connecticut?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Connecticut: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Connecticut

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Connecticut

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Connecticut:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Connecticut)$2,000 - $3,500$4,000 - $6,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Connecticut has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Connecticut.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Delaware? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/delaware.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Delaware?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Delaware: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Delaware

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Delaware

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Delaware:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Delaware)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in District of Columbia? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/district-of-columbia.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in District of Columbia?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in District of Columbia: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › District of Columbia

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in District of Columbia

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for District of Columbia:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (District of Columbia)$2,000 - $3,500$4,000 - $6,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in District of Columbia

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In District of Columbia, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $2,000 - $3,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $4,000 - $6,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in District of Columbia has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Columbia.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for District of Columbia is approximately $99,636 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $2,368 - $3,938 in District of Columbia.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review District of Columbia exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in District of Columbia?

Total Chapter 7 cost in District of Columbia is typically $338 filing fee plus $2,000 - $3,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in District of Columbia?

Chapter 13 in District of Columbia costs $313 in filing fees plus $4,000 - $6,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in District of Columbia?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Maryland ·
Virginia

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Means Test Calculator ·
District of Columbia Exemptions ·
How to File in District of Columbia ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Florida? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/florida.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Florida?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Florida: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Florida

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Florida

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Florida:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Florida)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Florida

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Florida, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Florida has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Middle District, Southern District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Florida is approximately $69,876 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Florida.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Florida exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Florida?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Florida is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Florida?

Chapter 13 in Florida costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Florida?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Georgia?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Georgia: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Georgia

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Georgia

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Georgia:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Georgia)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Georgia

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Georgia, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Georgia has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Middle District, Southern District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Georgia is approximately $65,628 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Georgia.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Hawaii? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/hawaii.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Hawaii?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Hawaii: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Hawaii

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Hawaii

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Hawaii:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Hawaii)$2,000 - $3,500$4,000 - $6,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Hawaii

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Hawaii, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $2,000 - $3,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $4,000 - $6,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Hawaii has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Hawaii.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Hawaii is approximately $86,427 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $2,368 - $3,938 in Hawaii.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Idaho? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/idaho.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Idaho?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Idaho: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Idaho

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Idaho

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Idaho:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Idaho)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Idaho

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Idaho, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Idaho has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Idaho.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Idaho is approximately $65,394 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Idaho.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Idaho exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Idaho?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Idaho is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Idaho?

Chapter 13 in Idaho costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Idaho?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Montana ·
Wyoming ·
Utah ·
Nevada ·
Oregon ·
Washington

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Illinois? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/illinois.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Illinois?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Illinois: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Illinois

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Illinois

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Illinois:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Illinois)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Illinois

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Illinois, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,500 - $3,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,500 - $5,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Illinois has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Central District, Southern District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Illinois is approximately $74,430 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,868 - $3,438 in Illinois.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Illinois exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Illinois?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Illinois is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,500 - $3,000 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Illinois?

Chapter 13 in Illinois costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,500 - $5,500 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Illinois?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Iowa ·

Kentucky ·

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Indiana? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/indiana.html

Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Indiana?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Indiana: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Indiana

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Indiana

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Indiana:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Indiana)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Indiana

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Indiana, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Indiana has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Southern District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Chapter 13: $313

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Indiana is approximately $63,399 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Indiana.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Iowa)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Kansas is approximately $69,197 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Kansas.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Kansas exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Kansas?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Kansas is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Kansas?

Chapter 13 in Kansas costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Kansas?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Nebraska ·
Missouri ·
Oklahoma ·
Colorado

Discharge Screener ·
Means Test Calculator ·
Kansas Exemptions ·
How to File in Kansas ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Kentucky? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/kentucky.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Kentucky?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Kentucky: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Kentucky

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Kentucky

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Kentucky:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Kentucky)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Kentucky

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Kentucky, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Kentucky has informal fee standards. Districts: Eastern District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Kentucky is approximately $58,632 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Kentucky.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Kentucky exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Kentucky?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Kentucky is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Kentucky?

Chapter 13 in Kentucky costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Kentucky?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Louisiana? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/louisiana.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Louisiana?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Louisiana: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Louisiana

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Louisiana

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Louisiana:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Louisiana)$1,000 - $2,000$2,500 - $4,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,368 - $2,438$2,843 - $4,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Louisiana

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Louisiana, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,000 - $2,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $2,500 - $4,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Louisiana has informal fee standards. Districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,368 - $2,438$2,843 - $4,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Louisiana is approximately $57,528 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,368 - $2,438 in Louisiana.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Chapter 13 in Louisiana costs $313 in filing fees plus $2,500 - $4,500 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Maine? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/maine.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home

Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Maine: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Maine

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Maine:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Maine)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438
