Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Maryland?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Pennsylvania ·
Delaware ·
Virginia ·
West Virginia ·
District of Columbia

Means Test Calculator ·
Maryland Exemptions ·
How to File in Maryland ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Massachusetts? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/massachusetts.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Massachusetts?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Massachusetts: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Massachusetts

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Massachusetts

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Massachusetts:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Massachusetts)$2,000 - $3,500$4,000 - $6,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Massachusetts

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Massachusetts, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $2,000 - $3,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $4,000 - $6,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Massachusetts has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Massachusetts.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Massachusetts is approximately $95,727 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $2,368 - $3,938 in Massachusetts.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Massachusetts exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Massachusetts?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Massachusetts is typically $338 filing fee plus $2,000 - $3,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Massachusetts?

Chapter 13 in Massachusetts costs $313 in filing fees plus $4,000 - $6,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Massachusetts?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

New Hampshire ·
Vermont ·
New York ·
Connecticut ·
Rhode Island

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Means Test Calculator ·
Massachusetts Exemptions ·
How to File in Massachusetts ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Michigan? [2026] -- BK Cost

Attorney Fees

Chapter 13 Cost

By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Michigan?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Michigan: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Michigan

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Michigan

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Michigan:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Michigan)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Michigan

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Michigan, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Michigan has informal fee standards. Districts: Eastern District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Michigan is approximately $66,087 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Michigan.

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Minnesota)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Mississippi? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/mississippi.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Mississippi?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Mississippi: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Mississippi

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Mississippi

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Mississippi:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Mississippi)$1,000 - $2,000$2,500 - $4,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,368 - $2,438$2,843 - $4,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Mississippi

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Mississippi, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,000 - $2,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $2,500 - $4,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Mississippi has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Southern District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Missouri)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

Attorney Fees in Missouri

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Missouri has informal fee standards. Districts: Eastern District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Missouri is approximately $64,972 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Missouri.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Missouri exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Missouri?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Missouri is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Missouri?

Chapter 13 in Missouri costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Missouri?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Iowa ·
Illinois ·
Kentucky ·
Tennessee ·
Arkansas ·
Oklahoma ·
Kansas ·
Nebraska

Discharge Screener ·
Means Test Calculator ·
Missouri Exemptions ·
How to File in Missouri ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Montana? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/montana.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Montana?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Montana: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Montana

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Montana

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Montana:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Montana)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Montana

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Montana, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Montana has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Montana.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Montana is approximately $66,501 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Montana.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Montana exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Montana?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Montana is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Montana?

Chapter 13 in Montana costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Montana?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Nebraska)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Nevada? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/nevada.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Nevada?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Nevada: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Nevada

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Nevada

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Nevada:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Nevada)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New Hampshire? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/new-hampshire.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New Hampshire?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in New Hampshire: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › New Hampshire

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in New Hampshire

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for New Hampshire:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (New Hampshire)$2,000 - $3,500$4,000 - $6,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in New Hampshire

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in New Hampshire has informal fee standards. Districts: District of New Hampshire.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $2,368 - $3,938 in New Hampshire.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review New Hampshire exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in New Hampshire?

Total Chapter 7 cost in New Hampshire is typically $338 filing fee plus $2,000 - $3,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in New Hampshire?

Chapter 13 in New Hampshire costs $313 in filing fees plus $4,000 - $6,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in New Hampshire?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Maine ·
Vermont ·
Massachusetts

Means Test Calculator ·
New Hampshire Exemptions ·
How to File in New Hampshire ·
File Without a Lawyer ·

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New Jersey? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/new-jersey.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New Jersey?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in New Jersey: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › New Jersey

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in New Jersey

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for New Jersey:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (New Jersey)$2,000 - $3,500$4,000 - $6,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in New Jersey

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In New Jersey, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $2,000 - $3,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $4,000 - $6,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in New Jersey has informal fee standards. Districts: District of New Jersey.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$2,368 - $3,938$4,343 - $6,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for New Jersey is approximately $93,051 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $2,368 - $3,938 in New Jersey.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review New Jersey exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in New Jersey?

Total Chapter 7 cost in New Jersey is typically $338 filing fee plus $2,000 - $3,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in New Jersey?

Chapter 13 in New Jersey costs $313 in filing fees plus $4,000 - $6,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in New Jersey?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

New York ·
Pennsylvania ·
Delaware

Means Test Calculator ·
New Jersey Exemptions ·
How to File in New Jersey ·
File Without a Lawyer ·

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New Mexico?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in New Mexico: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › New Mexico

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in New Mexico

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for New Mexico:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (New Mexico)$1,000 - $2,000$2,500 - $4,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,368 - $2,438$2,843 - $4,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in New Mexico

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In New Mexico, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,000 - $2,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $2,500 - $4,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in New Mexico has informal fee standards. Districts: District of New Mexico.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,368 - $2,438$2,843 - $4,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Chapter 13: $313

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for New Mexico is approximately $57,528 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,368 - $2,438 in New Mexico.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New York? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/new-york.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home

Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in New York?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in New York: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › New York

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in New York

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for New York:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (New York)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in New York

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In New York, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,500 - $3,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,500 - $5,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in New York has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Southern District, Eastern District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for New York is approximately $73,272 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,868 - $3,438 in New York.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in North Carolina? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/north-carolina.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in North Carolina?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in North Carolina: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › North Carolina

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in North Carolina

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for North Carolina:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (North Carolina)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in North Carolina

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In North Carolina, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in North Carolina has informal fee standards. Districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in North Dakota?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in North Dakota: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › North Dakota

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in North Dakota

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for North Dakota:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (North Dakota)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in North Dakota

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In North Dakota, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,500 - $3,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,500 - $5,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in North Dakota has informal fee standards. Districts: District of North Dakota.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for North Dakota is approximately $73,749 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,868 - $3,438 in North Dakota.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review North Dakota exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in North Dakota?

Total Chapter 7 cost in North Dakota is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,500 - $3,000 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in North Dakota?

Chapter 13 in North Dakota costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,500 - $5,500 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in North Dakota?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Montana ·
South Dakota ·
Minnesota

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Means Test Calculator ·
North Dakota Exemptions ·
How to File in North Dakota ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice.

Chapter 13 Cost ·

By State

A project of the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit (determination pending).

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Ohio? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/ohio.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Ohio?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Ohio: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Ohio

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Ohio

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Ohio:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Ohio)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Ohio

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Ohio, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Ohio has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Southern District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Ohio is approximately $63,564 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Ohio.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Ohio exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Chapter 13 in Ohio costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Oklahoma?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Oklahoma: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Oklahoma

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Oklahoma

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Oklahoma:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Oklahoma)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Oklahoma

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Oklahoma, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Oklahoma has informal fee standards. Districts: Northern District, Eastern District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Chapter 13: $313

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Oklahoma is approximately $60,636 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Oklahoma.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Oregon? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/oregon.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Oregon?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Oregon: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Oregon

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Oregon

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Oregon:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Oregon)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

Attorney fees (Pennsylvania)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Pennsylvania is approximately $71,547 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in Pennsylvania.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Pennsylvania exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Pennsylvania?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Pennsylvania is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,200 - $2,500 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Pennsylvania?

Chapter 13 in Pennsylvania costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,000 - $5,000 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Pennsylvania?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

New York ·
New Jersey ·
Delaware ·
Maryland ·
West Virginia ·
Ohio

Pennsylvania Exemptions ·

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Rhode Island? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/rhode-island.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Rhode Island?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Rhode Island: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Rhode Island

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Rhode Island

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Rhode Island:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Rhode Island)$1,500 - $3,000$3,500 - $5,500

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in Rhode Island

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In Rhode Island, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,500 - $3,000, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,500 - $5,500.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Rhode Island has informal fee standards. Districts: District of Rhode Island.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,868 - $3,438$3,843 - $5,913Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Filing Fees and Fee Waivers

Federal bankruptcy filing fees are set by the Judicial Conference and apply nationwide:

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Installment payments: You can request to pay the filing fee in up to four installments over 120 days. File Form 103A with your petition.

Fee waiver (Chapter 7 only): If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline and you cannot pay even in installments, you can request a complete fee waiver using Form 103B. The court has discretion to grant or deny the waiver.

Chapter 13 filing fees cannot be waived, but the fee can be paid in installments.

Credit Counseling and Education Costs

Two required courses add $30-$100 to your total cost:

Credit counseling (pre-filing): $15 - $50. Required within 180 days before filing. Must be from a DOJ-approved provider. Online courses take 60-90 minutes.

Debtor education (post-filing): $15 - $50. Required before discharge. Different from credit counseling. Covers budgeting and financial management.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Hidden Costs to Budget For

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Tax return preparation: You must provide recent tax returns to the trustee. If you are behind on filing, tax prep costs extra.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for Rhode Island is approximately $77,358 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,868 - $3,438 in Rhode Island.

Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure, strip junior liens, and cramdown car loans -- benefits worth far more than the filing costs.

The alternative -- wage garnishment, bank levies, lawsuits, and compounding interest -- typically costs far more over time.

Review Rhode Island exemptions to understand what property you can protect.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Rhode Island?

Total Chapter 7 cost in Rhode Island is typically $338 filing fee plus $1,500 - $3,000 in attorney fees, plus $30-$100 for required courses. Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Rhode Island?

Chapter 13 in Rhode Island costs $313 in filing fees plus $3,500 - $5,500 in attorney fees. Attorney fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Can I get the filing fee waived?

Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guideline can request a fee waiver using Form 103B. Chapter 13 fees cannot be waived but can be paid in installments.

Are bankruptcy attorney fees negotiable in Rhode Island?

Attorney fees vary by district, case complexity, and attorney experience. Get quotes from at least three attorneys. Some charge flat fees while others charge hourly. Ask about the total cost including all filing fees and expenses.

What are the hidden costs of bankruptcy?

Hidden costs include credit counseling ($15-$50), debtor education ($15-$50), tax preparation if you are behind on returns, replacement pay stubs, and potential costs for asset valuation or appraisals.

Bankruptcy Costs in Nearby States

Massachusetts ·
Connecticut

Means Test Calculator ·
Rhode Island Exemptions ·
How to File in Rhode Island ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in South Carolina?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in South Carolina: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › South Carolina

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in South Carolina

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for South Carolina:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (South Carolina)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Attorney Fees in South Carolina

Attorney fees are typically the largest cost of bankruptcy. In South Carolina, Chapter 7 attorney fees generally range from $1,200 - $2,500, while Chapter 13 fees range from $3,000 - $5,000.

Factors that affect what you will pay:

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in South Carolina has informal fee standards. Districts: District of South Carolina.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.

Cost Comparison: With Attorney vs. Pro Se

Filing MethodChapter 7 TotalChapter 13 TotalProsCons

With attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413Professional guidance, fewer errorsHigher cost

Pro se (without attorney)$368 - $438$343 - $413Lowest costComplex forms, no legal advice

Filing pro se eliminates attorney fees but requires you to complete all forms yourself, navigate court procedures, and attend hearings without representation. Chapter 13 pro se cases have very high dismissal rates. Pro se filing guide.

Many agencies waive or reduce fees for filers who cannot pay. Ask about fee waivers when you sign up.

Credit report copies: Free from annualcreditreport.com, but some attorneys charge for pulling and analyzing your reports.

Document preparation: If filing pro se, form preparation software costs $50 - $300.

Paycheck stubs: You must provide 60 days of pay stubs. Employers sometimes charge for replacement copies.

Vehicle appraisals: May be needed if you have vehicle equity to protect.

Real estate appraisals: May be needed for homestead exemption calculations.

Cost-saving tip: The means test threshold for South Carolina is approximately $61,050 for a single filer. If you are below this, you pass Part 1 of the means test automatically, which can simplify your case. Check your eligibility.

Is It Worth the Cost?

For most people drowning in debt, bankruptcy provides enormous financial relief relative to its cost:

A typical Chapter 7 eliminates tens of thousands of dollars in credit card debt, medical bills, and personal loans for a total cost of $1,568 - $2,938 in South Carolina.

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com

By State

Home › States › South Dakota

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (South Dakota)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Tennessee? [2026] -- BK Cost
Source: https://howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com/states/tennessee.html

howmuchdoesbankruptcycost.com
☰

Home
Filing Fees
Attorney Fees
Chapter 7 Cost
Chapter 13 Cost
Fee Waivers
By State

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Tennessee?

Complete cost breakdown for Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Tennessee: filing fees, attorney fees, credit counseling, and pro se options.

Home › States › Tennessee

Total Cost of Bankruptcy in Tennessee

The total cost depends on your chapter, whether you hire an attorney, and case complexity. Here is the breakdown for Tennessee:

Cost ComponentChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (Tennessee)$1,200 - $2,500$3,000 - $5,000

Credit counseling$15 - $50$15 - $50

Debtor education$15 - $50$15 - $50

Total with attorney$1,568 - $2,938$3,343 - $5,413

Total without attorney (pro se)$368 - $438$368 - $438

The filing fee is set by federal law and is the same in every state. Attorney fees vary by market, case complexity, and district norms.

Case complexity: Business cases, asset cases, and cases with litigation cost more.

District norms: Each bankruptcy district in Tennessee has informal fee standards. Districts: Eastern District, Middle District, Western District.

Attorney experience: Board-certified bankruptcy specialists may charge more.

Payment structure: Chapter 7 fees are typically paid upfront. Chapter 13 fees can often be paid through the repayment plan.

Watch for: "No money down" Chapter 13 firms may charge higher fees that are rolled into your plan. Always ask for the total fee, not just the upfront cost. In some districts, high-volume firms charge fees at or above the court-approved maximum while providing minimal individual attention.
